concerns: Transformations are conceptually clear but often lack operational detail (who exactly would implement T1–T5, statutory text examples, budgetary implications, enforcement mechanisms, timelines).
Some causal inferences rest on non-Hungarian studies; the package flags this uncertainty but does not supply Hungary-specific quasi-experimental evidence (necessary for decision confidence).
Cost and fiscal impact estimates (net savings, transport offsets, staffing implications) are discussed qualitatively but absent quantitatively, limiting short-term budgeting decisions.

Stakeholder mapping and political feasibility analysis is present in high-level form but needs concrete actor incentives and veto points (e.g., ministerial incentives, party politics, church maintainers) to be decision-operational.

A few referenced items use URNs or terse summaries; while auditable, the package sometimes presumes the reader will accept the cited study’s generalizability rather than unpacking key methods/limitations.
strengths: Clear mapping of a policy option space (T1–T6) linked to specific governance levers and trade-offs.
Extensive, auditable citation practice: inline finding IDs consistently map to appendix claims with URIs/URNs.
Explicit separation of empirical uncertainty and value/political choices; includes concrete research questions likely to change decisions.
Cross-national comparisons and historical precedents are used defensibly to highlight mechanism risks (e.g., Poland, Finland, Scotland).
Dilemmas are clearly stated, with attention to distributional effects (Roma, rural disadvantage) and implementation constraints (tankerület structure, transport responsibility).
